Paul opens Colossians 3 by anchoring everything that follows in a staggering reality: you have been raised with Christ. This is not metaphor or aspiration—it is your actual identity as a believer. Because you died with Him and your life is now hidden with Christ in God, the old patterns that once controlled you no longer have legitimate authority over you. This is why Paul can command you to put to death what belongs to your earthly nature with such force. He is not asking you to achieve something you lack; He is calling you to kill what you have already died to. The sexual immorality, greed, anger, and malice listed here are not minor character flaws—they are expressions of rebellion against God that invite His wrath. Paul reminds you that you used to walk in these ways, but that was before your resurrection with Christ. The person who lived for earthly desires is dead. The question is whether you will live as though you believe it.

What makes this chapter revolutionary is not the list of vices to avoid or virtues to cultivate, but the foundation on which all behavior stands: you have put on a new self that is being renewed in the image of its Creator. Christ is all and is in all. This means the abolition of every status that once divided people—Greek or Jew, slave or free, barbarian or Scythian. In Christ's body, these distinctions lose their power to determine your worth or your place. The compassion, kindness, humility, gentleness, and patience Paul commands are not strategies for getting along; they are the natural expression of a people remade in the image of God and bound together by Christ's forgiveness of them. You are called to forgive one another as the Lord forgave you—not because forgiveness is easy or because the other person deserves it, but because Christ's forgiveness of you is the measure and the model.

The peace of Christ should rule your hearts like an umpire makes the final call. Let His word dwell in you richly, not as occasional inspiration but as the governing force in how you speak, teach, and relate. Notice that Paul then moves to specific relationships—wives and husbands, children and fathers, slaves and masters. He does not treat these as separate from the spiritual realities He has just described. Your submission as a wife, your love as a husband, your obedience as a child, your work as a slave—all of it is to be done as for the Lord, in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father. Even in the ordinary relationships that shape daily life, even in work that feels mundane or unjust, you are serving Christ. You are not laboring for human approval or earthly reward, but for an inheritance from the Lord. This transforms everything. It means no hidden grumbling, no effort only when watched, no different standard when no one sees. The Lord Christ sees, and He is your true master.

Paul closes with a sober reminder: whoever does wrong will be repaid for his wrong, and there is no favoritism. Grace is not license. The resurrection you have been raised into calls you to a costly obedience that spans every relationship, every word, every deed. The life you now live—the one hidden with Christ in God—must visibly reshape how you treat your spouse, your children, your work, and those around you. When Christ appears in glory, you will appear with Him, fully revealed in that resurrection to which you already belong. Until then, let that future reality remake your present choices.
